title: 2025/09/26 - JU [Part 2]
description: Friday 26 September 2025 | Justice Committee Electoral Amendment Bill Submitters: Veronica Culpan Margot Govers Citizens Advice Bureau NZ -Kerry Dalton, Chief Executive New Zealand Public Service Association Te Pukenga Here Tikanga Mahi (the PSA) -Andrew McCauley, Policy Advisor -Fleur Fitzsimons, National Secretary Jonathan Clark Matthew Hart Christchurch Youth Council Incorporated -Chloe Dilley -Dean Banks -Thomas Richards -Lucas Roper Toitu Te Tiriti Otautahi -Teresa Butler, Activator Hazel Armstrong Rebecca Merrington Jean Hera John Lawson Nicki Spring Christopher Hawkins Peter Duncan
descriptionSummary: Electoral Amendment Bill. Veronica Culpan. Margot Govers

chunks.text: Are you able to hear me clearly? Yes, we can. Fantastic. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. Thank you to the members of the committee, to the submitters and to the parliamentary staff and to the public at large. I'm here before you, not just as a New Zealander, but as someone deeply concerned about the ethical implications of this bill and what it signals about our relationship to democracy itself.

The bill does not just merely adjust procedure, it redraws the boundary between who is included and who is excluded in the democratic process. It proposes more restrictive registration requirements. It introduces new barriers for some all in name of strengthening integrity of electoral law, and ensuring resilience of electoral system and making sure that the wrong people don't vote while increasing access by others, by increasing their donation threshold in the name of reducing the compliance burden.

But I ask you, integrity for whom and resilience from what timeliness. The advice is all that. This is not going to help us determine results more quickly and reducing the compliance burden for whom? For political parties, for lobbyists, for donors, because what this bill does intentionally or not is make it harder for some to vote, particularly Maori, particularly young people, transient, vulnerable, C, committing communities, the elderly, those who already face obstacles within the system

while increasing access undo cover for others. And it does it using the soft language of administrative reform that this bureaucracy can be just as effective as anything else in putting up a barrier. And silence risks. Disenfranchisement. The right to vote is a privilege not to be earned.

It is a right. Is it a condition of political existence? It's how we say I am here. I count, I matter. I shape the future alongside others. I shape the future to which I belong. And to make voting more difficult for anyone is to reduce people to objects acted upon rather than act subjects acting in the world.

This country was the first to grant women to vote a legacy that we rightly honor. But that honor means nothing if we allow the franchise to slowly be made conditional again to erode it. And we know that history does not only move forward, it also retrieves quietly, procedurally through legislation like this.

And we must decide whether to dist stand in defense of our legacy or to allow it to erode. Do we narrow representation or do we expand it? This is not just about policy, it is about principle. If ours is a democracy that fears or objects to the full participation of our people, all of us, is it a democracy?

Some people can vote, others cannot. Who decides? Is it worth defending some of the principles that really trouble me and how we are acting as a country right now? But I would now just turn briefly to a couple of snippets that highlight my political participation.

Punctuation marks throughout my life. We had political discourse over the family table, dining table at night. It doesn't sound very exciting when I say it that way, but we would've heated debates, we would grow up, um, talking about all manner of issues. It was robust and we would often agree on what the problems were, but we would disagree on what the solutions were.

We would have family excursions exciting time again to the voting booth and demonstrating that civic participation and action. I had that with my parents and I have demonstrated that with my son. We would wander on down, we would chat along the way, and we would not be the only people doing that.

And I can tell you, for anyone who's a parent or taken children on these things, you need to make it enticing and engaging. That's through stickers, through music, through laughter, making the voting environment a welcoming place. And you walk away with a sign that you have participated in something communal, that we are stronger together and you can see others around you.

And we need to demonstrate that to our children. So we need to make it welcoming to our children. Now turn now to something that I reflected on in my submission, which was a life admin hiccup with my parents. When they moved house having built, bought a new property well into their eighties and one, or they forgot to transfer the electricity bill that resulted in them being cut off only for a matter of hours, but it was easily done.

It was easily fixed because there was willingness. But if that had been them on their electoral role and they had turned up having voted and participated in the democratic process since they came of age, they would have been mortified. They would've been felt incredibly let down by the system who hasn't forgotten to update your details.

We have evidence of weaknesses in our system now just with the local body elections, with a number of candidates profiles being missed from booklets. Anecdotally, hearing about people who have fallen off one role or another. Not a huge problem at this point because people have becoming aware of it.

They can cast a special vote and all as well that is going to change. I urge you quite clearly and respectfully and unequivocally, please reject this bill. Do not let access to the vote be narrowed. Do not allow our subject to be reduced to paperwork.

Do not legislate away the dignity of democratic participation. We should be investing in our democratic processes. We should be investing in our people. Just make it open. Make it easy for all of us. Thank you. Alright, thank you for your, uh, your very nice presentation.

Unfortunately, we're way over time, but, um, thank you for joining us this afternoon. Okay, now I'm gonna call Margo, uh, Ms. Margo GOs to the front here. Very prompt. The addressed, I thought. Yeah. Yes. Trying to us back a hundred or so years. Cool. The floor is yours. We've got five minutes.

Just leave some time for any questions if you can. Ra chair and members of the committee in parliamentary staff, thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Margo Govers. Today I appear not as myself, but dressed in the spirit of Kate Shepherd. She and the woman's Christian Temperance Union at once published 10 reasons why Women Should Vote.

That Pamphlet, pamphlet helped to transform our democracy in the same spirit. I have my own 10 reasons. 10 reasons why we should not erect new barriers to voting. Due to some time constraints. I'll not cover them all, but I'll highlight some of the most important for ones that speak directly to the risks that this bill impose.

First voting is a right, not a favor. Voting is not a privilege to be rationed. It is a right guaranteed to every adult citizen by the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act. The Attorney General has already warned that this Bill unjustifiably limits that right with the risk of disenfranchising a great many New Zealanders.

And importantly, she also noted that there is no clear evidence. The counts would even be faster. If election day enrollments were abolished, we would pay a very heavy Democratic price with no guarantees of benefit. Second, the numbers are stark.

In 2023 election, around 450,000 people enrolled or updated their details during the voting period, including more than 110,000 on the election day itself. These were not ineligible voters. They were New Zealanders meeting every legal qualification.

The electoral commission's own report shows that while this created administrative pressure, the official results were delivered on time. The commission recommended administrative improvements, earlier, processing of special votes, digital roll markoff, and better resourcing. They did not recommend cutting voters off.

Third, working people need flexibility More than 220,000 New Zealanders have multiple jobs. They are shift workers, caregivers, commuters, people who are time poor for them, election day enrollment isn't procrastination. It's often the only practical way to participate to remove that option is to punish the busiest, the most stretched, and often the most vulnerable members of our democracy.

Fourth vulnerable groups will be hit hardest. It's not just the time pool who will be affected. The, the groups most likely to enroll late are young voters, Maori Pacifica, Asian communities, and renters. They are precisely the groups already under pressure around our democracy to set up new barriers that fall heaviest on them and is to deepen existing inequalities and political voice and representation.

Fifth trust is at stake. Public faith in elections depends on fairness and accessibility. If people believe the rules are designed to exclude them, then trust in the system itself erodes restricting access does not strengthen democracy. It corrodes it. And as the commission itself warned early cutoffs risk leaving voters frustrated and disillusioned, finally, this is an administrative issue, not a democratic one.

Special votes take longer to process. Both the governor, the attorney General, and the electoral commission are clear. Closing enrollments 13 days early does not guarantee a faster result. Yes, the number of voters using advance and special voting is increasing. That is a fact. And it does place additional strain on the count.

But the growth is a sign of a healthy democracy. More people participating, more people finding ways to cast their votes. The answer is to resource and modernize the system to meet the demand, not to cut people out of it. We must fix the, the count, not the franchise. As Kate Shepherd once said, all that separates whether race, class, creed, or sex is inhuman and must be overcome in 2025.

All that separates medi citizens from the, their vote will be an arbitrary 13 day deadline. That two must be overcome. So I urge the committee, honor the legacy of 1893, not unravel it in 2025. Protect the right to vote. Keep up democracy, open, fair, and universal.

Timely results are desirable, but universal suffrage is essential. Ra Well thank you for s um, presentation and thank you for coming in person. It's always more powerful when you turn up in person. Thank Question five, Mr. Chair. What catch you?

But obviously huge figure in our democracy. What do you think she would have to say? Uh, in terms of the franchise being trimmed as it is by this reform? I think she voted really hard for women and the, the, the WCTU as well, the voted really hard to get woman the vote.

Um, and I think that she would be quite dismayed to realize that now we are looking at cutting that vote simply because it's harder to count it. Okay. Alright. Thank you. Alright, thank you. Uh, now we've got, uh, citizens and Vice Bureau, uh, Ms. Uh, Kerry Dalton. Okay.
Good afternoon, Kerry. Um, we've got 10 minutes with you. Uh, so looking forward to your submission

Core Kerry Dalton ua. Um, so I'm the Chief Executive of Citizens of Vice Bureau and Citizens of Vice Bureau is a nationwide, locally based community organization that provides people with free, confidential, independent advice and advocacy.

We are based in 80 locations throughout New Zealand and our services provided by 2000 volunteers. We help anyone with any question or problem through our service. We strengthen democracy in ro New Zealand by empowering people to know what their rights and responsibilities are and options to act on them.

We also use what we learn from our clients to speak out for fear laws and policies and services, and to challenge those that have an adverse effect on people's rights or wellbeing. We strongly oppose two key provisions contained in this bill. Namely closing enrollment 13 days before election day and disqualifying all prisoners from who are serving a prisoners, a prison sentence from the right to vote.

Firstly, in relation to closing enrollment 13 days before election day. While we acknowledge the government's stated intent to improve administrative efficiency and electoral processes, the removal of same day enrollment and the shift to closing enrollment 13 days before election day risks undermining fundamental democratic rights.

It has the potential to disenfranchise a large number of eligible voters. The CAB is one of the places that people come to get help with enrolling on the electoral role. This is a contribution we make to ensuring everybody's ability to participate in a process which is crucial to democracy and fundamental to their rights.

Making the ability to participate as easy as possible is some something that everyone, especially our politicians, should strive to promote and protect. Therefore, it is very concerning and disappointing that this bill seeks to breed bring in a provision that is likely to limit participation in elections.

We note that this is in contradiction to advice from the Ministry of Justice and a warning from the Attorney General that it breaches the Bill of Rights. Hello? Yes, we're here. Oh, good. Suddenly you weren't we Missing intently? Excellent. Um, I was just saying that the Attorney General has, um, warned that it breaches the Bill of Rights and the Ministry of Justice states that it could result in lower enrollment rates, lower turnout, and more disallowed votes, and that it reduces ease for voters and creates a barrier to participation.

Protecting the right to vote and promoting measures for Democratic participation is too important to trade off for efficiency in vote counting, especially when the Attorney General, the Ministry of Justice, and as I understand it, the Electoral Commission itself have questioned whether it will increase the speed in which the final vote is counted.

So it will reduce participation in elections, but will not address the problem it's trying to solve. We recommend maintaining the status quo of the ability to enroll, to vote right up to and including election day in relation to prisoners' rights to vote.

We are concerned about and strongly oppose the Bill's proposal to introduce a blanket ban on people in prison being able to vote. Our volunteers provide the CAB service to people in prisons to ensure that they can get help in relation to their rights and responsibilities.

The CAB phone number is an approved number that people in prisons can call to get information and advice. Some of our people go into Taka Prison. We provide the service despite getting no funding for it to support people in prison because everyone has human rights and being treated with dignity and respect is more likely to lead to more positive sense of connection to community and society and support successful reintegration into society.

Um, and I just, um, in preparation for the submission, I talked to, uh, one of the people who goes into Reka Prison and he was actually the voting place manager, aka at the last general election. And, um, I asked him, um, for his insights and he said it was interesting to note that we took over 400 votes from a prison population, which was at 30 September, 2023, 715 made up of 362 people on remand and 353 who were sentenced the age of those

who voted ranged between their twenties to their sixties with most appearing to be in their thirties. The vast majority said that they had never voted before. Of those who said they'd never voted, most weren't on the electoral role. So their, um, the provision for them to vote, um, had a significant impact on their participation in voting system.

Um, we note that the Attorney General in her report on this bill found that a blanket ban on prisoner voting is inconsistent with the Bill of Rights. We also note that the treaty, the Wang Tribunal found that the previous blanket ban was a serious breach of Titi or Waitangi. Um, the Waitangi tribunal reached this finding because the ban disproportionately affected Maori who are overrepresented in the prison system as a result of systemic bias and social and economic disadvantage.

Wgi Tribunal recommended all restrictions on prisoner voting should be removed as all Maori have a treaty right to exercise the individual and collective by being able to exercise the vote in the appointment of their political representatives. From a democratic to and human rights perspective, the CAB opposes the blanket ban on prisoners' right to vote.

Okay. We support the view of the Waitangi Tribunal, the Ministry of Justice, and the Independent electoral review that all prisoners should be able to vote. Kerry, um, I'm just conscious we've got some questions. Sure. And you raised one issue that you haven't discussed, which is one no one else has discussed. So I'm going can I just, um, unless you've got something else, I'd really like to take the opportunity to ask you some questions. That'd be good.

Yes, that would be really good. I'd just would like to say one last thing. Um, I hope that this committee and the whole house in relation to this bill moves beyond partisan politics to principle politics and protects a fundamental tenant of our democracy, the right to vote, um, and keeps the elect the, the, the legitimacy of government of Parliament itself is based on voting in a general election.

The more inclusive the voting process, the greater the the legitimacy. Okay. Parliament? Yeah. Okay. I'll go to Mr. Rutherford first. Uh, Kerry, I'm just wondering if you can tell us which other countries we compare ourselves to that have, uh, same day enrollments, uh, up until election day, please.

Um, I'm sorry, I I don't have that information. Um, I can get it for you though. Okay. Um, what about, say Australia? Uh, one of our closest countries that we often compare ourselves to that low's enrollment, say for the 2019 federal election, 30 days before the election.

Um, I'm focused on our democracy and, um, how we would want it to operate in our country. And that's what I'm representing today. Okay. Can I ask you a question, Kerry? Because you, you've raised something that no one else has talked about, but in the context of enrolling to vote, central Elector Commission provides general multichannel options, right?

Yes. Now, I don't know whether you were listening, but we had the first, uh, GDES, and I was asking him the same question. He, he said the legislation is currently drafted uses words, the effect of some form of written communication. And he said under that arrangement, a text would probably be, uh, would meet that threshold.

Although I've asked them to provide more input on it. Are you saying your, in terms of your ability through your special specific channel tele, uh, telecommunications channel that you have, but are you saying that legislation is that currently drafted would preclude that? Because if it is, that's something we would be keenly interested in understanding?

Um, I don't think it precludes precludes it. Are you, it precludes, are you talking about precluding, um, In terms of voting and, and registering and doing all that sort of stuff On mine or on paper? Bill obviously doesn't deal with your, your good work that you do with pri providing advice, general advice to prisoners, but in, in the context of the voting, which is what this bill's about Yeah.

Is do you see any limitation around what you currently do or might do or you ought to do that you may not be able to do under the current draft of the bill? Um, I don't think so. I think that what we were raising was more what it empowers the electoral commission to do, which may mean that they put extra weighting on the online engagement.

Okay. That's Because of efficiencies? Yeah. As opposed To putting people's needs at the center of things. Well, we're actually more worried that the postal, they're gonna send stuff to old, uh, old postal addresses, run the new one thing. But if you see anything on that that you want to further submit on, I'd invite you to do that. That would be be really useful. Right.

Hey, thank you for your time today. Um, and it's good to hear from you. Yes. Good. Thank you. Thank you. Now we've got, um, members from the New Zealand Public Service Association. Is that correct? Good. We've got 10 minutes with you. Sorry, I'm just trying to keep us on track. Okay, No problem at all. Good afternoon. I'm flu at Simons.

I'm one of our national secretaries and I'm here with Andrew McCauley, our policy advisor. I just wanna thank you for, uh, hearing our submission, particularly on a Friday afternoon. We're keen. Good. Excellent. We should make some progress then. Um, it won't hopefully be a surprise to you, given you've got our submission that we're worried about the impact of this bill.

We think it is dangerous for democracy. We endorse the comments made by submitters that have appeared, uh, particularly the ones that have appeared just before us this afternoon. We agree with the Citizens Advice Bureau that if you put barriers in the way, it does start to question the legitimacy of decisions made, uh, in this building and in this, uh, environment.

We should only be looking for ways to encourage more participation, not put up barriers. What we've done in our submission is, um, we've taken a bit of an unusual approach for us in these submissions. We've actually gone to our members who work for the electoral commission and ask them for their input and advice. Yeah. And I hope you will appreciate that they have done that in a way that I think is quite constructive and useful for you.

You'll see that they've taken, uh, positions both opposing, uh, and supporting, but it's very important that workers through their union are able to bring their voice in their perspective to this table. Just wanna highlight some of the comments that they've made, uh, in particular around the need for greater resourcing of the work of the Electoral Commission.

Um, they've told us very clearly that they don't have sufficient resourcing to do their job well. Um, they've also told us around the need to scale up both throughout the electoral cycle on top of the core permanent staff and additional resources, for example, by elections, which we've had a few of.

Um, and the huge scaling up that's necessary during a general election to deal with the volume of work. But interestingly, uh, just reinforcing the comments that were made by the commission itself, they think that, um, the bill wouldn't address the issues that it's intended to address.

And I think some white should be given to the voices of the people who work at the commission when it comes to that. The commission itself said this yesterday, I think it was that they think our, our members, these workers think that the, uh, legislation ministers are, that ministers are underestimating the negative side effects that will have.

And, and particularly that it won't have the, um, that, that, that you, that there was a suggestion that there'll be a strain on the system to justify some of these changes. And the voice of the workers is that that's misplaced and, and not correct. And finally, just allowing some time for questions. I just wanted to read a couple of quotes from the workers.

The first one is that we have a gold standard electoral system, but it costs to fund it properly, politicians should see the system as something to be proud of. Not a burden to defund no other nation does as much to make voting as easy as we do, but you have to resource it properly. Another one, um, this I thought was a very good practical insight.

We lost a lot of long-term enrollment staff in 2022 when some regional offices were closed. When we ramped up in 2023 to respond to general election enrollments, we had lots of people who had never worked in electoral event before. Yeah. A lot of institutional knowledge walked out the door in 2022, and we paid the price in 2023.

And finally, because it's not all opposition. I did wanna pick up the point that you just raised with the Citizens Advice Bureau. Uh, removing postal requirement freezes up to explore other ways to get people information that will help them participate in democracy. Okay, cool. So we, there's A few questions we've got, Uh, Ms. Walters first, I've, uh, thank you Mr.

Chair, and thank you very much for the submission. This is a, a unique perspective from us. So that's for us that's really useful. I wonder whether you heard anything about a sense of, um, worry that there would be frustration from voters who for the last 30 years have expected to be able to register at least up till the day before the election.

And whether there is any sense that they would be facing potentially safety issues or certainly discomfort or having to deal with, um, deescalating people who are very frustrated that they can't register. Yes. I think you are picking up on a theme that came through with the feedback. Um, it's particularly around, um, these workers being very proud of being able to get voters out and not wanting to put, uh, barriers in place.

They talked about that the commission said the commission had been working harder, not smarter, and then they raised some concerns and it was really around those barriers that are put in place and the, um, lack of assurances that we won't see a suppression in voter turnout and enrollment. Okay. Cool. Hey, thanks. Thank you very much.

Um, that is, it is really good actually way to do a ion quite innovative. So. Great. Um, obviously you mentioned that they've been trying to tidy up and had the order to general's report that you're probably familiar with. Um, and I found that statement that, you know, we've got a gold standard electoral system and interest fund.

There's a sense of pride there. Um, when we had the electoral commission and I talked about the culture and values of the electoral commission and that they, they aim for greater enrollments and greater voter turnout. I guess my question is, from a worker's perspective, what will this step, which will have lower enrollments and lower voter turnout do to worker morale and culture?

I think it will go backwards. You are right to say they're very proud of their work. They are very proud of their work. They want the New Zealand's democracy to flourish and they want to enable as many people as want to and can to vote. So I think you're right to pick up on that as a, as a, as a matter that's likely to lead to reduce morale in that workplace and Okay.

Organization. No, Undoubtedly. Okay. Go to Ms. Paul And Kilda Andrew. Um, thanks for the submission. It's really helpful to have the, the voices and the realities of workers in front of us. Um, one question that I had was around the provisions around food and entertainment being offered.

I know in the past, the electoral commission have done a lot to try and entice particularly younger voters. I've seen that a lot at universities and just get, getting people enrolled and interested in what's going on. Um, did, did any of the people or workers that you talked to make any comments about those provisions that you might be able to share with us?

Um, we didn't specifically cover that part of the bill. We focused mainly on the, um, the parts relating to the workload of the commission around the 15 day enrollment period. Um, but yeah, not, not so much on the, um, on the treating and the kind of food voter voting places, But I think you are picking up on something which we know to be true as well, that those staff put a lot of energy.

And you can certainly feel it on, uh, university campuses, um, when they're around that there is a lot of, uh, pride and energy and a little bit of merch as well. Never goes a must and getting people engaged. I couple of, uh, just wanna pick up on the, um, issue around staffing in 2022. Did they explain why the electoral commission decide to get rid of staff and then have to rescale it up?

'cause that continuity and that expertise? It's like any job, right? There's a fair bit of specialist knowledge required. Did they say why the electoral commission got rid of staff back in 2022? Look, Successive governments haven't funded it properly. That's the reality. Is it, are you, are you, do you know for sure that's the reason why it happened 2022? Well, that's the, the consistent message that we've had from both the employer and the workers.

Okay. And that's not, uh, for lack of trying by both the employer and with support Of the workers. Okay. So just a fact. You may have heard yesterday, but, uh, electoral commission's got an extra 7.6 million that they noted yesterday for next year election. They've got money set aside for doing the business plan for the, uh, automatic enroll.
But hey, thank you for the submission, our very, it is a good, um, approach to take. Thank you very much. Thank you so much. Right. Cool. Right now we're moving into Jonathan Clark by Zoom,
retired educator. Are you here with us, Jonathan? Uh, yes. Um, oh, good. Yep. I, I'll, uh, just see if I can, I've got five minutes. Yeah. Okay. Uh, yep. Uh, Yeah. Uh,

my name is Jono Clark. I live in wha Thank you for the chance to speak. I'll use my time to urge you not to disenfranchise anyone. The sections of the bill, if enacted, will undermine participation in our democracy by some of the most marginalized members of our society, those who are furthest away from the levers of power.

Maximizing the opportunity for everyone to vote is one characteristic of a strong, healthy democracy. It is important to recognize that a person's vote is important not only to themselves, but for their children and for their grandchildren. I'd like to first talk about, uh, early closing of enrollment.

Current legislation demonstrates we can allow for people to register to vote up to and including election day. That is a win for democracy. The bill claims closing enrollment earlier will help to respond to the impact that enrollment processing pressures are having on the timeliness of the official count vote.

That to me, is a very weak argument at best when, uh, set against the loss of a person's ability to vote. There will be myriad reasons why people leave it until election day to register. There will be those who are facing major life challenges, people who are suffering transient, homeless.

Uh, undoubtedly there will even be people who are just hopelessly gloriously disorganized. They should not lose the, the votes to save the government a few bucks and a country a couple of weeks of uncertainty. There are are different ways to reduce the impact, perhaps by moving to a four year election cycle.

I'd like to speak now to the prisoner's right to vote. The bill casts the importance of the rule of law and civic responsibility as the primary reason to vote disenfranchise prisoners. But as the Attorney general right, honorable Judith Collins appointed out in her report the relevant clauses in this bill contravene the Bill of Rights.

Please heed her advice. The punishment for imprisonable crimes should be loss of liberty only thereafter the role of corrections within that, to quote Janice Adair, chief Inspector of the Office of the Inspectorate, should be to humanize and heal to help them on a pathway to wellbeing and empowerment.

Just be aware that at least 20,000 children in New Zealand have a parent and prison. This bill would take away their voice, their children's voice, their grandchildren's voice. The authors of this bill implicitly want to characterize prisoners as bad people.

We know most people and pe uh, end up in prison not for being bad, but due to, to, um, mental health problems, substance addiction, sexual and family violence, poverty, the many violences of colonization, other submitters will have impressed on you the numbers and their significance.

We also know that many bad people remain quite happily outside prison someon positions of considerable wealth and power. We need only to look across the Pacific Ocean to see that in a strong democracy, we want everyone to so-called good and bad to vote.

Voting should be compulsory, incentivized and celebrated. The voting age should be lowered to 16 old enough to have children old enough to vote. The only way to lose the right to vote should be to die. We should abolish the death penalty in, uh, we, we abolished the death penalty in 1989.

Let's not start heading back that way. Finally, I can see only perhaps one possible case for disenfranchising, uh, a section of our society. And this is light and symbolic. That group is around about the same size as the number of women in prison.

Perhaps parliamentary candidates should relinquish their right to vote. Perhaps they should surrender to the will of the people and quest for their party to grasp the levers of power. Let the rest of us decide whether they should serve and lead. Thank you for your time and attention.

Hey, thanks John for that, uh, wonderful stimulating thought. Thank you. Okay, we roll on. But thank you for, uh, being with us this afternoon right now. Matthew Hart by Zoom, hopefully. Are you there, Matthew?

Can't hear you. Matthew. I've seen Audio. How's that? Very good. We can hear you now. All right. Uh, hi. Um, thank you for allowing me to submit. Um, I am submitting as a individual. I am not actually affiliated with, uh, any groups, but, um, I am.

I am a New Zealand citizen and I was born in apartheid South Africa. Um, growing up during the end of that regime, I experienced how their propaganda worked. And while I am not an expert on propaganda myself, Jason Stanley is, uh, he's the chair in American Studies at the Munk School of Global Affairs and Policy in Toronto.

Uh, in his book How Propaganda Works, Stanley argues that political propaganda pretends to embody an ideal while hollowing out and damaging that very ideal. He goes on to show that flawed ideological beliefs hide the true effect of propaganda by reinterpreting the meaning of the original ideal.

So in other words, what I put to you is that this bull pretends to strengthen the integrity of electoral laws by preventing some people from voting in future elections. The only way that this could be true is if the goal of this bull is not democracy itself.

Um, and I don't want to rely on expert opinions because I'm sure you've heard many before, including the, uh, the Attorney General and many other former members of government and um, members of civil society. Um, but what I would like to do is just explain this in terms that may resonate more using vibes.

So in my opinion, most laws that made apartheids possible would not be passed today if they used the same language that they did back then. So if a member of the Apartheid National Party suddenly appeared as a member of New Zealand government of today, I feel like this electoral amendment bull is the type of bull they could write a bull that doesn't explicitly mention a targeted class, race or ethnic group, but one that removes their rights, nonetheless.

A bull that politically favors on democratic means of, um, of remaining in power, a bull that should be checked in the bin alongside the flawed ideology that that created it. Um, and I recommend replacing this ball with one that rather implements the recommendations of the independent electoral review of 2023 in full.

Right. Thank you Mr. Hart. Um, any questions from anyone? She All right. Thank you. Have you still there, Mr. Hart? Okay. Thank you for your submission. Right. Uh, we're going to go to the Christchurch Youth, uh, council Incorporated. Uh, is it Katie? I only saw her first name. Is it
right? Hello? I dunno all your names, but, um, I think we've got 10 minutes with you. Um, please leave some time for questions, but, uh, floor's. Yours

Um, the level of trust that we have in our democratic institutions and the voluntary adherence to conventions and norms by our political actors.

One such norm is that the significant changes to electoral law are only made by cross partisan consensus. And it is in some respects, a reflection of the golden rule.

Whatever else political parties might disagree on, there is a rec reciprocal expectation that the other party will not change the rules of the game to their own advantage. Without this understanding, New Zealand would be little different to authoritarian states like China, where there is no contest of power.

For this reason, I view any change to electoral law that does not have cross partisan agreement as potentially very dangerous. Um, and so I would like to ask that this change be abandoned. Thank you.
